data.title: Can The Markets Maintain Their Cool?
data.description:
If you exclude that three-week period in April when the most draconian tariff regime since the beginning of the twentieth century was announced and then promptly reversed (technically put on hold, but does anyone really believe that 46 percent tariffs are coming to Vietnam on July 2?), the first six months of 2025 have actually been pretty humdrum.

The S&P 500 is up about two percent from where it was at the beginning of January, while the 10-year Treasury note is hovering right around its average yield of 4.4 percent for this period.

Both stocks and bonds, in other words, seem to be registering a conviction that the worst of the potential economic hit is behind us, with a resumption of something like the cyclical trend that was prevailing prior to the whole tariff kerfuffle: a “soft landing” with growth slowing but remaining positive while inflation settles back to its two percent target. Are the markets correct in their composure, or are they premature in their assumption that all is well and back to the brandy?

Hard and Soft Converge

The optimists have a pretty good case to make simply on the basis of the available data. A few weeks ago, we were writing about the divergence between so-called hard and soft data. Surveys such as the Consumer Confidence index, the Michigan consumer sentiment report and the NFIB small business sentiment index all plummeted amid economic uncertainty among households and businesses.

The question we asked back then was whether the hard data, headlined by GDP growth, inflation and unemployment, was going to “catch up” with the doom and gloom vibes of the surveys. Now it seems like the trend might be going the other way.

The latest Consumer Price Index report came out this week, and it showed core CPI (excluding the volatile categories of food and energy) at 2.77 percent, its lowest level in four years (headline inflation, which includes food and energy, was even closer to the two percent target at 2.8 percent).

Last week’s employment report was also mostly fine, with payroll gains a bit better than expected and overall unemployment stable at 4.2 percent (though, as we noted in our report last week, the unemployment rate for recent college graduates is a potential source of concern).

Meanwhile, the surveys have been trending up. The latest Michigan sentiment report, fresh off the presses on Friday morning, registered a significant increase from last month and was well ahead of economists’ forecasts. The convergence, in other words, seems to be going in a positive direction.

Where’s the Inflation?

Is it surprising that higher inflation isn’t showing up? After all, despite the administration’s repeated backtracks from the direst of tariff threats, the overall tariff rate for US consumers is still at around 20 percent give or take, higher than it has ever been since 1934. The rational expectation would be that the higher tariffs beget higher prices. Yet, the month-to-month increase in prices from April to May was just 0.1 percent for both headline and core CPI.

The most likely explanation for the non-event of higher inflation to date is that many businesses ramped up inventories before the new tariffs took effect, and thus are able to sell their goods without undue price increases while maintaining stable profit margins.

Evidence for this can be seen in the massive drop in imports recently – a 16.3 decrease in April from the previous month. But inventories run out in due time. That is why many economists believe that inflationary pressures won’t really kick in for another month or two, but that consumer-facing prices are likely to be higher towards the end of the summer. Maybe it’s a good idea to get your back-to-school shopping done well in advance of Labor Day weekend.

Another Geopolitical Wild Card

The news this morning of Israel’s large-scale attacks on Iran, targeting nuclear facilities, military sites and the residences of prominent leaders adds another variable to the equation.

Generally speaking, markets are quick to regain equilibrium after unexpected geopolitical news breaks – witness for example the resumption in calm of commodity markets within days of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

The immediate market reaction on Friday is within the usual bounds – stocks down but not dramatically so, bond yields steady, and oil prices up sharply but retreating a bit from their overnight highs.

We don’t know yet the extent to which Israel intends to continue its attacks, or what its planned end game might be, but there is the potential that it could be a significantly destabilizing event.

The World Bank has already projected that global growth will slow in 2025 to 2.3 percent, which is half a percent lower than what the bank forecasted at the beginning of the year. That forecast is based on an assumption of oil at an average of $66 per barrel – so the implications of structurally higher prices for oil and other commodities do matter.

While we are impressed with the markets’ ability to remain cool, calm and collected in the face of all the first half uncertainty, there is still a long way to go before the end of the year, and plenty of challenges awaiting us around the next bend. This is not a time for relaxing one’s guard.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: QuinStreet: Time To Hold, Opportunity At Sight
data.description:
QuinStreet Inc. (NASDAQ:QNST) has appeared to be experiencing low growth in recent years. However, when analyzing its quarterly revenues, it shows the opposite result. With a possible chance of growth in the future, in my opinion, it is recommended to hold on to its shares at this time.

The company was founded in 1999 by Doug Valenti (current CEO), and is headquartered in Foster City, California. Operates as an online marketplace company which provides services in the financial and home sectors. The company's goal is to help its clients find a targeted audience. Therefore, the platform manages other private label virtual platforms, where consumers can compare services from different brands and prices.

The company only showed some profitability in the 2000s, starting in 2002 when it became profitable with its revenue of $13 million, and in 2010 after valuable acquisitions, it reached revenue of $334.835 million, making an offering on NASDAQ and thus having its shares publicly listed under the ticker QNST.

In my opinion, the strategy used to expand its presence in the market through strategic acquisitions of other service companies has worked in the past, and the following have generated relevant results:

  • CardRatings | 2008
  • Insure | 2009
  • MyBankTraker | 2019
  • Modernize | 2020
  • Entre outras

In 2008, they acquired the CardRatings website and Insure the following year, establishing their initial presence in the financial segment. In 2019, reinforcing this presence in the sector, they acquired the banking content website MyBankTraker. In 2020, they acquired the Modernize website for $67.5 million, executing their entry into the home services sector and expanding their portfolio to a new market. With this strategy, the company has been disengaging from less promising sectors in which it invested in previous years (such as education), and has been betting on digital sectors with higher growth (financial).

Last year, insiders have been selling their shares of the company, outpacing the volume of purchases, which may not be a good sign. CEO Doug Valenti and CFO Gregory Wong remain at the top of this list of stock sales.

Financial

When separating the annual revenue in a table, it does not appear to be promising as it shows low growth over the years. However, when separating it into quarters, it is possible to notice how it has been growing in recent months. Even though revenue has stagnated in the months of December and March, it may still be promising in the near future.

When divide the revenue value by the sectors in which it operate, it is notable how the financial sector is the most advantageous for the company, representing more than half of the total revenue. The strategic acquisitions made in previous years established its presence in this sector, generating the majority of its profit.

The company has competitors in the financial market with comparable or higher revenue, but in my opinion, it could have an advantage due to its diversification of sectors, opening up more sources of revenue, however, the other sectors which it operates have not been as profitable. While NerdWallet and BankRate work only with finance, offering credit and personal financial services, QuinStreet offers life and health insurance, loans, cards, mortgages, domestic and legal services, and searches for technical and higher education courses.

After 2017, BankRate stopped reporting its revenues publicly, so it is not possible to add it to the comparison.

Valuation

P/E, the consensus for 2026 is 27.86 PE given the consensus projected Earnings, a higher value than the sector which is 13.26, however, this value is balanced given the Growth Level in Net Income, and in Revenue that QNST has been presenting in the last 2 quarters. The Market currently prices states that a similar growth will continue to occur in the next 2 years, until the company reaches the value of $25.25 in its shares.

On a comparative basis, it continues with low P/S values ​​(0.8) and the industry average in P/B (3.84), and the general market view is that it will continue to grow, which is a good sign. After the company lost a large part of its insurance contracts in 2022 (given by a general "crisis" in the sector), losing a large percentage of its main consumers, who only started to hire again in June 2024, this being the reason for the 17.6% growth in its second quarter of this year.

This growth is expected to continue, as these are operational contracts, which although not long-term agreements, tend to be a relevant part of their clients operations. The notice period for the end of the contract is 30 days, revealing a certain risk and dependence on the insurance sector (the main source of revenue for the financial sector). The level of investment by insurers in marketing has not yet reached its historical peak, and is projected to do so, revealing potential for growth.

This is the market consensus, however, given that the company has had operating and net losses over the past 3 years, I would like to see a more pronounced takeoff, or improvement in gross margin before it becomes a solid and safe buy, given the great dependence and unpredictability of the financial sector. If in the next quarter, revenue continues to grow significantly and margins improve by a few points, I believe it would become a good buy recommendation.

Gross margin being the company's main Driver and Risk is the biggest indicator of profitability, historically following around 7.5% to 12%, it indicates the cost of advertising, given that insurers are willing to pay more for leads. Recently, management's base scenario is that gross margin will gradually increase from FY2024. Along with revenue, I would like to see more strength from the company before making a concrete recommendation. The number of leads may fall given a reduction in consumption of search engines along with the popularization of artificial intelligence and its search tools, and social media companies, knowing this, possibly charge more for leads within their platform.

Catalysts and Drivers

Most of the company revenue is currently generated in the US, but I believe there is a chance for the company to expand to the international financial market through acquisitions, as it has been doing nationally. In 2011, the company arrived in Brazil, and in 2020, the education company Quero Educação acquired its Brazilian operations. According to Quero Educação CEO, QuinStreet had more than 60 million hits in Brazil last year.

As mentioned earlier, another effective way to continue accelerating revenue growth is through strategic acquisitions of other companies at the national level, a practice that already shown results in previous years.

Risks

Since it is a website, the company depends on search engines (such as Google, Bing, Yahoo!, etc.), and therefore needs to be well-positioned on the first pages of search results to remain high, whether appearing organically or paid. In my opinion, if there is any change or major change in these engines, the number of daily visitors could decrease drastically, which in a certain period of time will make it difficult to attract an audience. The AI ​​searches that are being integrated can also be a risk to the company, since with these engines, users do not need to click on third-party links to find the result they are looking for, which causes a decrease in direct clicks on QNST website.

Conclusion

I believe that in the future the company may present itself as an advantageous purchase option, and therefore, it is recommended to hold the stock at this time, until it reach the next quarter of this year. By closely monitoring the Gross Margin indicators (a sign of traffic acquisition efficiency), and the continuity of revenue growth in the coming quarters, we will have confirmation of this.

data.title: Origin Bancorp: Too Early To Be Bullish On This One
data.description:
Over the last couple of years now, I have been spending more time analyzing financial institutions. The search for interesting businesses in this industry ultimately brought me to Origin Bancorp (NYSE:OBK), a decent sized bank with a market capitalization right now of $1.06 billion. The firm is interesting because it operates in some of the sunbelt states, which are the states that are growing the most rapidly at this point in time.

In recent years, management has achieved attractive growth from a deposit and loan perspective. But overall revenue and profitability has been mixed. Relative to the book value of the company, shares are actually priced at levels that I would consider to be appealing. But the stock is more expensive than I would like it to be compared to earnings. On top of this, asset quality falls short of what I would like to see. So because of these reasons, the best thing that I can do is rate it a ‘hold’ for now. Having said that, investors should certainly keep a close eye on the business to see what might change moving forward.

Digging into Origin Bancorp

Based out of Louisiana, and with roots dating back to 1912, Origin Bancorp is most certainly an interesting business. During its lifetime, the enterprise has been successful in establishing itself in key regions of the southern portion of the US. At the end of the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the company had no fewer than 55 locations spread across parts of Texas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, and even Florida.

From these locations, the firm has provided a wide array of banking products and services. These include deposit accounts, but it also involves certain lending activities like originating single and multifamily mortgages, providing funds for residential construction and commercial buildings, making loans to small and medium-sized businesses, and providing consumers with miscellaneous loans as the firm deems appropriate.

On top of this, the company provides customers with certain commercial and personal property and casualty insurance products through its own wholly owned insurance subsidiary that has been in operation for over 35 years. It also engages in other activities. As an example, it issues business credit cards, provides customers with safe deposit boxes, helps to facilitate bank transfers, and even offers personal financial management solutions.

One of the things that makes the company really interesting to me is its location. Although I mentioned that it operates in the sunbelt states, management widens this market to focus solely on the southern portion of the US. April 1st of 2020 through July 1st of 2024, the region saw net migration of 2.82 million. That compares to a Net outflow from the west of 968,751, from the Midwest of 528,601, and from the northeast of 1.32 million. At this time, Texas, as a standalone economy, operates as the 8th largest economy on the planet. And in the one-year ending in January 2025, it saw more jobs created than any other state. South Alabama and the Florida Panhandle, both of which the company operates in, have also seen attractive growth. And unless something changes in the foreseeable future, this kind of expansion can be expected to continue.

The balance sheet for the company has similarly grown. From 2022 through 2024, deposits expanded from $7.78 billion to $8.22 billion. There was a further rise to $8.34 billion in the first quarter of 2025. Even though the company is based out of Louisiana, it is worth noting that its greatest exposure is to Texas. In fact, the company has 29 of its branches in the Lone Star State. This has resulted in a whopping 55% of all of its deposits coming from Texas alone. Louisiana accounts for only 38%, while Mississippi is responsible for the rest.

As deposits have increased, loans have increased as well. From 2022 through 2024, the value of loans for the company grew from $7 billion to $7.48 billion. There was then an increase to $7.49 billion in the first quarter of 2025. Speaking of loans, 71% in total are dedicated to the state of Texas. So in essence, the company is taking deposits from Louisiana and allocating that capital toward Texas, where it views that the growth opportunity is greater.

Management makes sure that these deposits are well diversified. The largest chunk involves the 27% that is dedicated to commercial and industrial loans. It is worth noting that the company has only a small portion of its total loan portfolio dedicated to office assets. I always worry about this kind of exposure whenever I look at a bank because of rising vacancy rates nationwide. But according to management, only $370 million, or 4.9%, of the firm's loan portfolio is dedicated to them. 26% of this figure involves medical assets, which tend to be more stable than other types of office properties. It is worth noting that the company does have a whopping 20% of its loan portfolio dedicated to residential real estate and consumer loans. And it also has exposure to other types of properties as well.

Even as deposits and loans have grown, some parts of the balance sheet have gotten smaller. Primarily, I'm talking about securities. They have declined from $1.66 billion in 2022 to $1.12 billion last year. In the first quarter of 2025, they did tick up to $1.18 billion. But that still represents a substantial decline in recent years. The good news is that the firm has used this opportunity to grow its cash and cash equivalents balance from $359 million to $486.2 million. Meanwhile, debt has plummeted from $841 million to only $102.1 million. I would say this is all a fair trade-off.

With the company's balance sheet growing, we have seen an increase in net interest income. Even as the firm's net interest margin contracted from 3.42% to 3.22%, there was a rise in net interest income over the last three years from $250.6 million to $292.9 million. And for the first quarter of 2025, the $75 million that the company generated beat out the $70.3 million reported last year. That growth was also attributable to an improvement in the company's net interest margin from 3.19% to 3.44%.

Unfortunately, not all income statement items improved. Over the last three years, non-interest income has remained fairly consistent, ranging between $55.4 million and $58 million. The unfortunate thing is that we did see some weakening from the first quarter of 2024 to the first quarter of 2025. During this window of time, non-interest income contracted from $17.3 million to $15.6 million. This was in spite of the fact that insurance commissions and fee income for the company grew from $7.7 million to $7.9 million, and was driven by two primary factors. First, there was a substantial reduction in mortgage banking income from $2.4 million to $0.9 million. And there was also a $1.8 million worsening limited partnership investment income and losses. The reduction in mortgage banking income was because of the firm's decision to sell almost all of its mortgage servicing rights last year. Actual origination revenue remained fairly flat, which is surprising considering how difficult the housing industry is right now.

Even as net interest income for the company has grown, overall profitability for the company has actually worsened. Because of rising costs, including significantly higher salaries and employee benefits expenses, increased occupancy and equipment costs, higher office and operations expenses, and more, net profits fell from $87.7 million in 2022 to $76.5 million last year. And in the first quarter of this year, the $22.4 million in net profits generated by the company fell short of the $22.6 million reported the previous year.

When it comes to valuation, the picture for the company is definitely mixed. In the chart above, you can see that it's trading at a price to earnings multiple of 13.9. This is above the 6 to 10 range that I like to see, and is higher than four of the five companies I compared it to. The good news is that, on a price to book and price to tangible book basis, the shoe is on the other foot. In the chart below, you can see that the company is trading at around book value and tangible book value. Furthermore, only one of the companies is cheaper than it is on a price to book basis. Two of them are cheaper on a price to tangible book basis.

I would be more accepting of the decline in profitability and the high price to earnings multiple if Origin Bancorp enjoyed high asset quality. But that's not what we have here. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets compared to the return on assets of these same five companies I have compared it to already. The reading of 0.93% that it has is a bit below the 1% minimum threshold that I like to see. It's also lower than three of the five companies I compared it to. In the subsequent chart, you can see that the return on equity reading of 7.79% places the firm below four of the five competitors. That also places it meaningfully below the 10% minimum that I like to see.

The good news for those who are bullish about the company is that management is working hard to achieve improvements in profitability. It is currently working on a massive optimization initiative spanning almost all parts of the business, including its cash management operations, dealing with debt, making changes at its physical locations, and more. This is a long-term initiative, with some parts of it expected to be completed this year. In all, management expects annualized cost-savings of at least $23.4 million, but the total amount could be larger.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, Origin Bancorp is an interesting firm that does seem to have some potential based on its geographic location. Having said that, the picture just doesn't look good enough in my opinion to justify a bullish assessment. Shares of the company are cheap relative to book value and tangible book value. But they are expensive relative to earnings. Asset quality is low, and I don't like the continual decline in profitability that we have seen in recent years. Given these factors, I believe that rating it a ‘hold’ is the most sensible thing I can do right now.

data.title: Lyft: In A Very Tough Setup
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a hold rating for Lyft, Inc. (NASDAQ:LYFT), as I think the underlying business is showing some genuine signs of improvement, but the structural headwinds remain too large to ignore. Momentum looks solid on the surface, but when we consider the intensity of competition, especially from Uber, and the recent Delta partnership loss, I think it’s hard to underwrite meaningful upside near-term. Execution will be key, and until LYFT proves it can win sustainably, I prefer to stay on the sidelines.

Business description

LYFT’s core business is ridesharing, which connects riders and drivers through its app. They also offer services such as a shared bike and scooter network in select cities, along with Express Drive (a car rental program for drivers). The core business is very much like what Uber (UBER) offers, so that is one way readers can think of LYFT. Beyond its core, some areas that LYFT has expanded into include Lyft Business, subscriptions, and advertising through Lyft Media.

Operational Momentum and Strategic Initiatives

LYFT is clearly gaining momentum operationally, as 1Q25 active riders grew 11% y/y to 24.2 million and total rides were up 16% y/y to 218.4 million. Notably, ride growth is outpacing user growth, with ride frequency per user reaching a five-year-high, which suggests that engagement has improved meaningfully. This is a very healthy early sign of the LYFT platform becoming more sticky and paints a very positive demand outlook.

Importantly, my view is that this momentum can continue, as the improvements were driven by structural reasons. In simple terms, the product got better and is working. For instance, LYFT recently launched Lyft Silver (a new offering that targets older adults), which, I thought, was smart, as this is actually a growing user segment but underserved. Another good example is Price Lock. This is particularly important because it directly addresses one of the biggest pain points for frequent riders: price volatility from surges. By giving users price certainty, LYFT can build stronger loyalty. The numbers do back this claim: Price Lock memberships grew 21% in 1Q25 vs. 4Q24, and retention is running >70%. Some may think that this is bad because it lowers average revenue per ride, but in my view, it’s a worthwhile trade-off. Stickier users are more valuable over time. All these may seem incremental, but collectively, I expect them to help LYFT deepen loyalty on both sides of the marketplace.

Apart from the internal developments, the one strategic initiative that could be really exciting is the planned acquisition of FREENOW. This will bring LYFT into nine European countries. For what it's worth, LYFT has demonstrated to a good extent that it can operate outside the US—LYFT Canada saw 55% y/y ride growth in 1Q25. But what makes FREENOW particularly interesting is that it’s not just about geographic expansion; it is a direct and effective entry into a large, fragmented, and under-digitized market. The thing about Europe is that about half of the market still runs offline, and taxis are often reached through traditional phone dispatch. That creates a large and tangible opportunity for LYFT. And there are real synergies to unlock. LYFT can go in and layer in its tech, pricing models, and marketplace expertise to improve FREENOW’s performance directly.

If executed well, this could support both near-term growth and extend LYFT's long-term growth runway.

Enduring Headwinds and Market Risks

That said, there are still meaningful headwinds that make this a “show-me” story. The most immediate challenge is ongoing pricing pressure in the US (gross bookings per ride fell 3% y/y). It does beg the question of whether lower pricing was the reason for the volume growth, and if so, it may not be sustainable. The reason provided was that there was a mix shift toward lower-priced international rides and affordability-driven features like Price Lock, but I think it is difficult to ascertain this, as the reality is LYFT is still facing huge competitive pressure from UBER. UBER is by far the largest player and way ahead of LYFT. On top of that, LYFT also faces competition from autonomous players like Waymo. These don’t mean that LYFT will necessarily lose; it just means that LYFT needs to invest a lot more in rider and driver incentives to stay competitive, and I am not sure if LYFT has what it takes to win a war of attrition. UBER has a cash balance of $6 billion and adj EBITDA of $8.5 billion (estimate for FY25e), which is way higher than the $2 billion cash balance and ~$500 million adj. EBITDA (estimates for FY25e) that LYFT has. The discrepancy in the balance sheet is even larger if we compare it to Google (which owns Waymo). Moreover, LYFT recently lost its partnership with Delta Air Lines, which I see as a massive setback as it reduces LYFT’s scale.

As for the FREENOW acquisition, as I said above, it looks really good on paper. But there are real execution risks to consider, which may hinder the timing of this showing positive impacts on the books. Europe is a collection of multiple countries, and each has their own regulatory and operational complexity. What works in one may not work in the other, and synergies may not be extracted as easily as LYFT expects.

Valuation

I think there are very justifiable reasons for LYFT to continue trading at a discount to UBER—9x vs. 19x forward EBITDA. UBER has a larger scale, better profitability, and faster growth than LYFT. And not only that, LYFT is the one that is facing very strong competitive pressure, and it doesn’t have the same balance sheet strength to compete effectively. The loss of the Delta Air Lines partnership makes things worse. There are good things going on under the hood for LYFT, but I just don’t see the market attaching a lot of value to those “potentials” until the visible headwinds are being addressed.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for LYFT. There is a lot to like under the hood, but none of that changes the fact that LYFT is still facing a much larger competitor, facing ongoing pricing pressure, and dealing with a weaker balance sheet. At 9x forward EBITDA, the stock isn’t expensive, but it’s cheap for a reason. Until LYFT shows it can consistently grow share and defend margins, I don’t think the market will reward the upside optionality.

data.title: What's On Your (Alternatives) Bucket List?
data.description:
Introduction

Investors have in recent years sought to broaden their portfolios away from the traditional 60/40 allocation between stocks and bonds. This often entails shifting to something like a 50/30/20 portfolio, the last component of which serves as an “alternatives bucket.” There is no shortage of options with which to fill this bucket. But the “alternative” label often obscures the fact that many of these products do little to smooth the return profile of the resulting portfolio compared to the 60/40 starting point. Moreover, since the alternative bucket is usually relatively small, investors often miss the opportunity to stir it up with simultaneous allocations to multiple strategies.

Simplify launched the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF(CTA) in 2022. In addition to aiming for returns that are competitive with both stocks and bonds, CTA seeks to pass the litmus test of an alternative: a performance pattern that differs from equities and fixed income. Notwithstanding our confidence that CTA will continue to exhibit both these characteristics, similar to how diversification benefits stock and bond portfolios, we see value in diversifying within the alternatives bucket. In particular, the Simplify Currency Strategy ETF(FOXY), Simplify Target 15 Distribution ETF(XV), and Simplify Commodities Strategy No K-1 ETF(HARD) are ideally positioned to fill this role, or rather, to top off the bucket.

Alternative… to What?

There is a long list of assets that investment managers would like to convince investors are alternatives. That list includes commodities, gold, timber, real estate, private equity, private credit, artwork, and crypto assets, to name a few. A cynic might simply say an alternative is anything but plain vanilla stocks or bonds. Another angle may be the degree to which an asset exploits a risk premium that is not widely available in public equities or debt.

However, a common-sense approach might be to reorient the definition away from what it is to how it behaves, particularly in relation to the performance of stocks and bonds. In other words, as noted above, an “alternative” is an asset whose performance is appreciably different, perhaps one could say lowly or negatively correlated, to stocks and bonds. Using this simple criterion could both narrow and expand what merits consideration in the alternatives bucket.

As an example, private credit has recently come into the spotlight as an alternative for individual investors. Figure 1 shows the drawdown history of the classic 60/40 alongside that of the Indxx Private Credit Index. Even absent any proper statistical analysis, it seems clear that private credit might not be the optimal solution for investors looking for something to zig when stocks and bonds zag. The aim here is not to suggest private credit is a bad investment. But for purposes of filling the alternatives bucket, it might not be worth the pour.

Figure 1: 60/40 vs. Indxx Private Credit Index Drawdowns

August 2013 – May 2025

Figure 2: Return Correlations

May 2021 – May 2025

Less surprising: both broad commodity exposure and gold have solid diversification potential. And regular Simplify readers might have expected the negative correlation between 60/40 and the SocGen CTA Index, which is the benchmark for our own CTA.

Investors may also do well to consider things that might not immediately come to mind in a discussion about alternative investments. For example, the Bloomberg GSAM FX Carry Index—a benchmark comprised of currencies—is not only negatively correlated with stocks and 60/40, but its correlation with the SocGen CTA Index is fairly muted as well.

Of course, some things do not lend themselves to inclusion in the table above on account of lacking data, but investors would do well to consider them. Structured notes—already used by many in portfolio construction—can provide solid returns while exhibiting little in the way of co-movement with either core holdings or other alternatives.

One other thing to note: the representative index used in the private credit example above is comprised of actual manager results rather than the performance of an asset in a vacuum. If the bogey captures an average of funds, then logically, some managers are doing worse (perhaps by a lot) and some are doing better (also perhaps by a lot).

The same holds for many of the benchmarks used in the alternative space, which highlights another important consideration: manager selection matters a lot when it comes to alternatives. For example, we attribute the success of CTA directly to the philosophy, process, and people managing the fund. There are plenty of managed futures ETFs out there, but there is only one CTA.

Stirring the Bucket

Our client conversations suggest that many investors make an allocation to a single alternative. They thus forsake the benefits of diversifying over the many strategy types which could potentially fill their alternatives bucket. We believe that constructing a portfolio of alternatives—in the same way an investor might blend exposures over different markets and/or managers in the equity and bond portions of their portfolio—makes sense.

The four Simplify funds listed below collectively exemplify all of what we have discussed thus far. While their histories are in some cases short,1 our analysis suggests that their behavior provides attractive diversification not only with a 60/40 baseline, but relative to each other as well. This complementarity makes each an attractive candidate for inclusion in an investor’s alternatives allocation. And each is structured to provide attractive returns geared to risk premia which are often modestly represented, if at all, in traditional stock and bond investments.

  • Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF: Simplify’s flagship managed futures offering with a -0.4 correlation to 60/40 since its inception in March 2022 and a track record of positive convexity in risk-off environments. CTA employs a systematic approach to going long and short various commodities with daily rebalancing and no equity or currency exposure.
  • Simplify Currency Strategy ETF: Our recently launched systematic currency strategy that aims to harvest yield from the foreign exchange market. Separate modeling for DM and EM currencies accounts for differences in market structure while effectively capturing the distinct yet persistent behaviors of both. Volatility scaling aims to maintain an attractive return profile while guarding against drawdowns.
  • Simplify Commodities Strategy No K-1 ETF: Our long-biased commodities ETF that seeks to provide attractive returns alongside inflation hedging. Utilizes a risk-based allocation framework to reduce exposure when markets are showing signs of vulnerability.
  • Simplify Target 15 Distribution ETF: Simplify’s recently incepted approach to generating attractive monthly income through tightly risk-managed volatility selling. XV squarely targets an outcome rather than judging itself solely against the performance of a benchmark, providing income alongside anticipated modest correlations with other assets. We view XV as a compelling replacement for the structured notes investors may already be utilizing, but with numerous operational benefits in addition to its investment merit.

In Conclusion

Simplify advocates an allocation to alternatives as part of a well-diversified portfolio. The specific percentages will vary based on the circumstances of the investor. However, we believe it is important to exercise prudence on what to consider for inclusion in the alternatives bucket. Specifically, asset behavior—both realized and expected—is a more relevant consideration than simply whether something is labeled as an alternative. Moreover, diversification not just to alternatives but within them as well is an oft-overlooked opportunity.

We have highlighted four Simplify funds that we believe can empower investors to maximize the benefits of their allocation to alternatives. We look forward to connecting to discuss how we can help make them work for your portfolio.

1Inception dates: XV Apr 14’25, FOXY Feb 3’25, HARD Mar 27’23, CTA Mar 7’22

Glossary

Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return (BBg Cmdty): Composed of futures contracts and reflects the returns on a fully collateralized investment in the Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM.) BCOM is made up of 23 exchange-traded futures on physical commodities, representing 21 commodities which are weighted to account for economic significance and market liquidity. This combines the returns of the BCOM with the returns on cash collateral invested in 13 week (3 Month) U.S. Treasury Bills.

Bloomberg Gold Subindex Total Return (BBg Gold): Reflects the return on fully collateralized future positions.

Bloomberg GSAM FX Carry Index (BBg GSAM fx Carry): Provides an equal risk weighted exposure to the Bloomberg GSAM FX G10 and EM Carry strategies. These strategies rank the currencies using an average of recent implied US dollar interest rates as the carry measure.

Bloomberg US EQ:FI 60:40 Index (BBg 60/40): Designed to measure cross-asset market performance in the US. The index rebalances monthly to 60% equities and 40% fixed income. The equity and fixed income allocation is represented by Bloomberg US Large Cap (B500T) and Bloomberg US Agg (LBUSTRUU) respectively.

Contango: A situation where the futures price of an asset is higher than the spot price. This results in an upward-sloping forward curve.

Dow Jones US Real Estate Total Return Index (DJ US Real Estate): Represents REITs & other companies that invest directly or indirectly in real estate through development, management or ownership, including property agencies. The index is a subset of the Dow Jones U.S. Index, which covers 95% of U.S. securities based on float-adjusted market capitalization. Index was first calculated on February 14, 2000.

Foreign Exchange Market (FX): The global marketplace for the trading of one nation's currency for another.

FTSE Private Equity Buyout Index (FTSE PE Buyout): Measures the performance of the US private equity buyout industry through a combination of liquid and publicly traded assets. These publicly traded assets are allocated across seven sector portfolios, each representing a different sector in which US private equity buyout firms invest.

FTSE Private Equity Venture Capital Index (FTSE PE VC): Seeks to replicate the performance of the FTSE Venture Capital Benchmark Index through a combination of liquid, publicly listed assets. These publicly traded assets are allocated across seven sector indices, each representing a different sector in which US venture capitalists invest.

Indxx Private Credit Index (Indxx Pvt Credit): Tracks the performance of the Business Development Corporations (BDCs) and Closed-End Funds (CEFs), trading in the US, with significant exposure to private credit, as defined by Indxx.

Managed Futures: An investment where a portfolio of futures contracts is actively managed by professionals. Managed futures are considered an alternative investment and are often used by funds and institutional investors to provide both portfolio and market diversification.

Nasdaq Crypto Index (NASDAQ Crypto): Designed to measure the performance of a significant portion of the digital asset market and to provide a benchmark for institutional investment in this new and emerging asset class. The Index is specifically designed to be dynamic in nature, broadly representative of the market, and readily trackable by investors.

S&P 500 Total Return Index (S&P500): The S&P 500® is widely regarded as the best single gauge of large-cap U.S. equities and serves as the foundation for a wide range of investment products. The index includes 500 leading companies and captures approximately 80% coverage of available market capitalization. The S&P 500 Total Return Index is based on the price changes and reinvested dividends of the S&P 500.

SG CTA Index (SocGen CTA): Calculates the net daily rate of return for a group of 20 CTAs selected from the largest managers open to new investment. The SG CTA Index is equal-weighted and reconstituted annually and has become recognized as the key managed futures performance benchmark.

Structured Note: A debt obligation that also contains an embedded derivative component that adjusts the security's risk-return profile.

Volatility: A measure of how much and how quickly prices move over a given span of time.

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) before investing. To obtain an ETF's prospectus or Summary prospectus containing this and other important information, please call (855) 772-8488, or visit SimplifyETFs.com. Please read the prospectus carefully before you invest.

An investment in the fund involves risk, including possible loss of principal.

The fund is actively-managed and is subject to the risk that the strategy may not produce the intended results. The fund will also rely on the Futures Adviser’s judgments about the value and potential appreciation of particular securities which if assessed incorrectly could negatively affect the Fund.

The Fund’s use of futures may involve different or greater risks than investing directly in securities and the contract may not correlate perfectly with the underlying asset. These risks include leverage risk which means a small percentage of assets invested in futures can have a disproportionately large impact on the Fund. This risk could cause the Fund to lose more than the principal amount invested. Futures contracts may become mispriced or improperly valued when compared to the adviser’s expectation and may not produce the desired investment results. The Fund’s exposure to futures contracts is subject to risks related to rolling. Extended periods of contango or backwardation can cause significant losses for the Fund. Any short sales of the futures contracts by the fund theoretically involves unlimited loss potential since the market price of securities sold short may continuously increase.

FOXY: The Fund’s use of futures may involve different or greater risks than investing directly in securities and the contract may not correlate perfectly with the underlying asset. These risks include leverage risk which means a small percentage of assets invested in futures can have a disproportionately large impact on the Fund. This risk could cause the Fund to lose more than the principal amount invested. Futures contracts may become mispriced or improperly valued when compared to the adviser’s expectation and may not produce the desired investment results. The Fund’s exposure to futures contracts is subject to risks related to rolling. Extended periods of contango or backwardation can cause significant losses for the Fund. Any short sales of the futures contracts by the fund theoretically involves unlimited loss potential since the market price of securities sold short may continuously increase.

Investments linked to commodity or currency futures contracts including exposure to non-U.S. currencies can be highly volatile affected by market movements, changes in interest rates or factors affecting a particular industry or commodity. Changes in currency exchange rates can be unpredictable or change quickly which will affect the value of the Fund.

HARD: Short-term performance, in particular, is not a good indication of the fund's future performance, and an investment should not be made based solely on returns.

HARD: Investments linked to commodity or currency futures contracts including exposure to non-U.S. currencies can be highly volatile affected by market movements, changes in interest rates or factors affecting a particular industry or commodity. Changes in currency exchange rates can be unpredictable or change quickly which will affect the value of the Fund.

HARD: Repurchase Agreement Risk. The Fund's investment in repurchase agreements may be subject to market and credit risk with respect to the collateral securing the repurchase agreements.

data.title: Walgreens Boots Alliance: Voting 'For' Is The Best Remaining Option
data.description:
It has been almost half a year since I published my last article about Walgreens Boots Alliance (NASDAQ:WBA) in early January 2025. And while the stock increased almost 22% in the meantime, which is a very good performance (with Walgreens clearly outperforming the S&P 500), this is only a secondary matter now. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

The major bullish argument we can make at this point is the extremely low stock price with almost every possible negative scenario being priced in (aside from bankruptcy, which seems rather unlikely with an Altman Z score of 2.27 and a balance sheet with reasonable debt levels).

I would argue that we are very close to the bottom and the stock won't decline much further. Of course, we never know what might happen, but the stock jumping quickly to $15 or $20 is a real possibility. On the other hand, we should also not be surprised when the stock is dropping further.

From a fundamental point of view, Walgreens Boots Alliance does not seem like a good investment, as the company is not profitable right now and is also struggling to grow the top line. And the U.S. Healthcare segment, once the segment with high hopes for growth, is also struggling to grow at a high pace. And dividend investors should be very cautious as the risk of Walgreens Boots Alliance suspending or cutting the dividend again is rather high.

But now it seems like a stock jump to $15 or $20, which is described as a possibility in my last article, won’t happen as Walgreens Boots Alliance will most likely get acquired by Sycamore Partners.

In the following article, we will look at the acquisition and what it implies for shareholders. And at a first impulse, we could assume that quarterly results are not important for shareholders anymore as shareholders will get a fixed price per share if the acquisition goes through. However, on a second look, it is still important as shareholders can receive up to $3.00 per share in additional payments when certain criteria are met. Before we take a closer look at the acquisitions, let’s start by looking at the last quarterly results.

Quarterly Results

On April 8, 2025, Walgreens Boots Alliance reported second quarter results, and we only got limited information this time as the company did not hold an earnings call and also did not release an investor presentation.

While the company did beat earnings per share and revenue estimates, the results were horrible once again. Sales increased from $37,052 million in Q2/24 to $38,588 million in Q2/25 resulting in 4.1% year-over-year growth. But aside from the top-line growing, Walgreens Boots Alliance had to report an operating loss once again. Compared to an operating loss of $13,171 million in the same quarter last year, the company had to report an operating loss of $5,567 million. And although we have to point out that an impairment of goodwill had a huge negative impact on results ($3,653 million in Q2/25), the company would have also reported an operating loss even without the goodwill impairment as selling, general and administrative expenses exceeded the gross profit. Walgreens Boots Alliance also reported a diluted loss per share again – instead of a loss of $6.85 in Q2/24, the loss per share was $3.30 in this quarter. Free cash flow was also negative once again – in Q2/24 free cash flow was negative $610 million and in Q2/25 it was $418 million in negative free cash flow.

Additionally, we can look at the three different segments and U.S. Retail Pharmacy increased its sales from $28,861 million in the same quarter last year to $30,380 million in this quarter (resulting in 5.3% year-over-year growth). Usually, companies report the operating income, but Walgreens Boots Alliance is reporting its gross profit instead and a decline of 4.7% year-over-year to $5,301 million.

The International segment increased its revenue slightly (0.6% year-over-year) to $6,060 million, while gross profit declined a little bit from $1,287 million in Q2/24 to $1,255 million in Q2/25 – resulting in a 2.5% year-over-year decline.

The third and final segment is U.S. Healthcare and in my last article I already wrote:

In the last two years, Walgreens Boots Alliance was especially focusing on U.S. Healthcare, which is probably the business segment with the biggest hopes for growth in the years to come. But when looking at the reported results, we still see an increasing revenue, but growth rates are slowing.

In the meantime, the performance of the segment got even worse. The good news is that gross profit increased from $191 million in Q2/24 to $346 million in Q2/25, but sales declined from $2,176 million in Q2/24 to $2,152 million in Q2/25 – resulting in 1.1% year-over-year decline. While CareCentrix sales increased 6.5% and Shields sales increased 29.7%, VillageMD sales declined 6.2% year-over-year. This reflects lower fee-for-service and risk-based revenue at VillageMD. It is also including the impact of clinic closures.

Acquisition

But the major news in the last few months was the potential acquisition by Sycamore Partners. On March 6, 2025, Walgreens Boots Alliance entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired. The company will be taken private and WBA shareholders will receive $11.45 per share in cash. The transaction is expected to close in the fourth quarter of calendar year 2025.

I don’t really want to start a discussion whether Walgreens Boots Alliance is a good fit for Sycamore Partners, as retail is certainly part of their portfolio, but expertise in healthcare is certainly lacking. The important question is what this means for shareholders.

On July 11, 2025, Walgreens Boots Alliance shareholders will vote on the acquisition in a special meeting (it is also possible to vote per mail or phone). The Board of Directors has recommended that shareholders will vote for the proposal.

Although it is expected that the deal will go through, let’s look at the scenario of shareholders mostly voting against the proposal. In such a scenario, Walgreens Boots Alliance will remain a publicly traded company, but it seems likely that the stock price will decline steeply in such a scenario. Especially when considering the last reported results and the huge losses – and especially U.S. Healthcare struggling – investors probably don’t have much confidence in the future of Walgreens Boots Alliance right now.

If shareholders vote for the proposal, they will receive at least $11.45 per share in cash. But under the terms of the acquisition, shareholders will be able to participate in the future monetization of divested assets via the Divested Asset Proceed Rights (or DAP Rights). Shareholders can receive up to $3.00 per share, and this might lead up to $14.45 per share in total payments. In a presentation, three potential scenarios for additional value creation are outlined.

The additional cash shareholders can receive will stem from a potential sale of Village MD, which might lead to net proceeds of up to $4.0 billion and Walgreens shareholders will receive 70% (but limited at $3.00 per share or around $2.7 billion).

It was also announced that Stefano Pessina, who is Chairman of Boots right now and was also CEO until 2020, almost doubled his stake from previously 17% ownership of Walgreens Boots Alliance to about 30% and therefore holding almost one third of the private company. Additionally, speculations exist about Boots also becoming an independent company. However, the parent company has tried to sell Boots in the past, which did not work out as the offers made were not good enough.

Decisions to Make

Investors must decide right now what to do with their shares and how to act. And in my opinion, three scenarios are possible:

  • Scenario I: Investors can sell the stock right now and will get about $11.35 per share and therefore almost as much as investors will get at least when the acquisition is going through.
  • Scenario II: A second scenario is to vote for the acquisition and when the acquisition is going through, investors will get at least $11.45 and up to $3.00 in additional payments under certain circumstances.
  • Scenario III: A third and final scenario is to vote against the proposal and hoping for Walgreens Boots Alliance performing better as listed and stand-alone company.

In my last article I explained that I see the potential for a turnaround in the business and explained that it might make sense to hold the shares and wait. Back then, it was a possibility to bet on the potential of higher stock prices following a turnaround of the business (while taking on the risk of Walgreens Boots Alliance declining further – the business and the stock).

However, not, the decision to make is a little different. The possibility of better on a better performance by the business and therefore a higher reward for shareholders (due to a rising stock price) is not given anymore. Instead, investors must make one of the three decisions outlined above and while the theoretical potential for huge upside potential is no longer on the table, investors now can vote for getting at least $11.45 per share and, in a best-case scenario, $14.45 per share. And at least for almost everyone who bought in the last twelve months, this is a good deal.

Everybody else will lose money and has no chance of ever getting back his or her investment. However, when looking at the last reported results, we see that Walgreens Boots Alliance is continuing to struggle in a major way. Free cash flow recently turned positive again after it was below zero for several quarters, but the company is now reporting a loss per share for several quarters in a row.

Maybe $14.45 is the best deal we can get for the business, and maybe hopes for a turnaround were too optimistic after all.

Conclusion

I personally will hold on to the Walgreens Boots Alliance shares I still have (I have sold a part of my position in the past already) and wait for the decision by the shareholders in July 2024. I assume that shareholders will vote for the acquisition, and we will hopefully receive $14.45 for the outstanding shares – under the current conditions, this seems like the most favorable scenario. This will also be the closing chapter for one of the worst investments I ever made, with me losing more than 50% of my invested capital. But of course, everybody holding on to the shares and waiting for the shareholder’s vote must be prepared for the stock to crash in case of the shareholders voting against the acquisition.

On June 26, 2025, Walgreens Boots Alliance will report third quarter results for fiscal 2025, but considering the pending vote by shareholders, we can assume that the results won’t have much impact on the share price. But the results might have an impact on investors’ decision. If results are not great (once again), shareholders might rather vote for the acquisition, which might increasingly seem like the best scenario for Walgreens Boots Alliance at this point.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Chime Leads As IPO Market Heats Up With More Big Listings
data.description:
Six IPOs and three SPACs debuted this week. Four IPOs and four SPACs submitted initial filings.

Mobile banking platform Chime Financial (CHYM) priced its IPO above the range to raise $864 million at an $11.4 billion market cap. Chime says it has 8.6 million Active Members using its suite of products, with 67% of them relying on Chime to serve as their primary financial relationship as of March 31, 2025. The company believes this positions it as its members' central financial hub. In the first quarter of 2025, its Active Members used Chime for an average of 54 transactions per month, of which 75% were purchase transactions using Chime-branded debit and credit cards. The company had previously raised funding at a $25 billion market value. Chime’s revenue growth accelerated to 32% in the 1Q25, although the majority of its revenue comes from economically sensitive consumer spending. Chime finished the week up 29%.

Space and defense company Voyager Technologies (VOYG) priced its upsized IPO above the range to raise $383 million at a $1.9 billion market cap. The company’s business spans three segments: Defense & National Security, which provides communications technologies, guidance, navigation and controls, signals intelligence, and defense systems; Space Solutions, which delivers space infrastructure, advanced space technology, science systems, and mission services; and Starlab Space Stations, which is a commercial space station planned to succeed the ISS. Key partners and customers include Palantir, NASA, Lockheed Martin, the US Air Force, and Sierra Space. The company has a $900 million ceiling IDIQ contract with the U.S. Airforce, although it is significantly unprofitable, and its loss margin worsened in the 1Q25. Voyager finished the week up 75%.

SMB-focused insurer Ategrity Specialty Insurance (ASIC) also priced above its price range to raise $113 million at a $822 million market cap. Ategrity is a specialty insurance company dedicated to providing excess and surplus products to SMBs across the US. As of March 31, 2025, the company had a network of 512 distribution partners, providing increased transaction opportunities and diversified sources of business. It offers insurance solutions for property (40% of gross written premiums in 2024) and casualty (60%), It had total assets of $1.1 billion and equity of $398 million at year-end. Its gross written premium increased 24% in 2024, although it depends on its largest shareholder for investment management. Ategrity finished the week up 39%.

Aerospace and defense company AIRO Group Holdings (AIRO) priced well below its range to raise $60 million at a $263 million market cap. AIRO’s business is organized into four operating segments: Drones, Avionics, Training, and Electric Air Mobility. The company saw strong growth and margin improvement in 2024, though it relies heavily on government customers for revenue. AIRO soared 140%.

Singapore-based shipbroker Vantage (VNTG) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $13 million at a $125 million market cap. The company acts as an intermediary between oil companies, traders, shipowners and commercial managers. It helps facilitate contract negotiations and deliveries, provides consulting and operational services, and provides additional services related to demurrage and claims settlements. Vantage finished the week up 8%.

Hong Kong-based apparel supplier 707 Cayman Holdings (JEM) priced its US UPO at the bottom of the range to raise $10 million at an $88 million market cap. The company provides various solutions to apparel brands, including market trend analysis, design and product development, sourcing, production management, quality control, and logistics to customers. 707 Cayman finished the week up 41%.

Three SPACs also listed: Blue Water Acquisition III (BLUWU) raised $220 million to target healthcare, Blue Acquisition (BACCU) raised $175 million to target manufacturing and data centers, and BEST SPAC I Acquisition (BSAAU) raised $55 million to target the consumer goods sector.

While not included below, leading global meat products producer JBS (JBS) began trading on the NYSE in a dual-listing in the US and Brazil, as part of a restructuring. It ticked up 2% from its opening price.

Week Ahead

Two sizable IPOs are scheduled to price in the short holiday week ahead.

Precision medicine platform Caris Life Sciences (CAI) plans to raise $400 million at a $5.2 billion market cap. The company is developing and commercializing a platform combining next-gen sequencing and AI/ML technologies for cancer diagnostics. Growing but highly unprofitable, Caris is targeting a large opportunity in the dynamic but increasingly crowded oncology market.

Florida-based insurer Slide (SLDE) plans to raise $320 million at a $2.2 billion market cap. Led by the founder and former CEO of Heritage Insurance, Slide operates in the residential property insurance market. It focuses on single-family homeowners, condominium owners, and other commercial residential properties, primarily on the coasts of Florida and South Carolina, which are exposed to significant hurricane risk.
